prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 52-year-old woman.
My blood pressure spikes when I forget my pills--I just took them now so it should come back down.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I checked my blood pressure because I've had a mild headache today and it was very high. I don't have chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking.
- History: I haven't been on blood pressure meds before.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You had a very high blood pressure reading with a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or speech trouble. That pattern often means immediate emergency (a hypertensive emergency) is less likely, especially if your pressure falls after medication. Still, new or very high readings deserve prompt evaluation to start or adjust treatment and check for organ strain. Recheck your blood pressure after resting and after the medication takes effect. Go to the ER right away if you develop chest pain, severe or sudden headache, sudden vision change, weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, or if readings are ≥180/120 or do not come down with treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You had a very high blood pressure reading with a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. That pattern can sometimes be a temporary rise, especially if it responds to medication, but a new or very high reading deserves prompt evaluation to prevent complications. Because you report it spiked and you just took pills (and there’s contradictory history about prior blood pressure meds), recheck your blood pressure when settled, note any repeat very high readings or new symptoms, and arrange medical follow-up soon so treatment and causes can be reviewed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You describe a very high blood pressure reading with only a mild headache and no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or speech problems. That pattern makes an immediate life‑threatening emergency less likely, but a very high reading still needs prompt medical attention to confirm the reading, assess for risks or organ effects, and begin appropriate treatment. Rest, recheck the blood pressure after 5–10 minutes, and record readings. If readings stay very high or symptoms worsen (severe headache, chest pain, breathlessness, vision loss, weakness, numbness, slurred speech), seek emergency care right away.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your very high home blood pressure plus a mild headache but none of the red-flag symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, trouble speaking) makes a life-threatening emergency less likely right now. Because you said you just took medication and the reading may fall, rest and recheck your blood pressure in 15–30 minutes. If it comes down and you feel fine, you still need prompt medical follow-up to confirm readings and start or adjust treatment. If the high readings persist or you develop chest pain, severe or worsening headache, breathing trouble, vision problems, weakness, numbness, confusion, or slurred speech, seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You had a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or speech trouble. You said you just took your pills, so your pressure may come down. When blood pressure is very high without signs of organ damage, it’s usually not an immediate life‑threatening emergency, but it does need prompt medical assessment. Recheck your blood pressure in about an hour and again later today. Seek urgent care or the ER now if the reading remains very high or you develop chest pain, trouble breathing, worsening headache, vision changes, weakness, or speech problems.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a very high blood pressure reading with only a mild headache and none of the red‑flag symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness or speech trouble). That pattern is less suggestive of a life‑threatening hypertensive emergency, especially since you say you just took medication and the pressure may fall. Still, a new or severe BP elevation needs timely evaluation to confirm readings, check for organ effects, and start or adjust treatment. Recheck your blood pressure a short time after the pill, rest, and seek prompt medical follow‑up to arrange assessment and ongoing treatment. If you develop worsening headache, chest pain, breathlessness, vision change, focal weakness, confusion, or if BP stays very high, get emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You reported a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, or speech problems. That pattern makes a life‑threatening emergency less likely right now, but a single very high reading still needs prompt follow‑up because untreated severe hypertension can cause organ damage over time. Rest, recheck your blood pressure after the medication you just took and again later today. If readings fall into a safer range and you remain symptom‑free, outpatient evaluation is reasonable. If blood pressure stays very high or you develop chest pain, breathlessness, fainting, sudden vision changes, weakness, or slurred speech, seek immediate care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You describe a very high blood pressure reading with a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, or speech problems. That pattern suggests an urgent problem (needs prompt evaluation) rather than an immediate life‑threatening emergency right now. Very high blood pressure can cause symptoms and can damage organs over time, so it’s important to have the level rechecked, basic tests done, and treatment started or adjusted if needed. If new severe symptoms develop (chest pain, severe breathlessness, sudden vision loss, focal weakness, slurred speech) go to the ER immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 65%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You had a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, or trouble speaking. A single high reading can be from stress or missed medication and isn’t necessarily an immediate life‑threatening emergency when there are no other symptoms, but new or very high blood pressure still needs prompt evaluation because it can damage the heart, brain, kidneys over time. Rest, recheck your blood pressure after 15–30 minutes (and keep a log of readings), and monitor symptoms. If readings come down and you feel well, arrange urgent outpatient follow‑up to start and adjust treatment. Seek emergency care if symptoms develop or the blood pressure stays extremely high despite medication.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You describe a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, or trouble speaking. That lowers the chance of immediate life‑threatening complications. Still, very high readings can damage organs over time or suddenly; it’s appropriate to re‑check your pressure after resting 5–10 minutes and again after your medication dose. If the high reading persists or the headache worsens or you develop chest pain, shortness of breath, vision problems, weakness, numbness, or speech changes, go to the ER. Even without those symptoms, you should get prompt medical evaluation to adjust treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%
